title: Zero-Shot Vessel Segmentation on Angiography: SAM 3 Text-Prompting with a Custom Candidate-Scoring Selector
affiliations: Division of Vascular Surgery, Penn State Milton S. Hershey Medical Center, Hershey, PA
stage: In progress · preliminary results — external validation on the Dr.SAM benchmark (n=500); carotid/TCAR evaluation ongoing.

abstract.Objectives: Automated vessel segmentation on digital subtraction angiography (DSA) has historically required anatomy-specific, supervised models trained on hand-labeled data, limiting generalization across vascular territories. We tested whether a general-purpose, text-promptable segmentation foundation model (SAM 3) can segment opacified arteries on DSA zero-shot — with no fine-tuning, no per-anatomy retuning, and only natural-language prompts — and whether a lightweight, anatomy-agnostic post-processing layer can convert that capability into accurate masks.
abstract.Methods: SAM 3 (facebook/sam3) was evaluated in three prompt arms — text-only (a fixed three-prompt "smart-fallback" ladder: "arteries on angiogram", "opacified arteries on x-ray", "arteries on x-ray"), bounding-box-only, and combined text+bbox — with model weights frozen throughout. External validation used the Dr.SAM benchmark (Zohranyan et al., CVPR Workshops 2024): 500 pelvic-iliac DSA images with hand-traced ground-truth masks and bounding-box metadata for 1,500 vessel segments, stratified into stenosis (n=77), aneurysm (n=61), and normal (n=382). Per-image Dice was computed against the ground-truth traces. A classical, anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring layer (gates plus a soft quality score over geometric/intensity features, normalized within each image's own candidate pool) was then applied post-hoc to the cached SAM 3 candidate masks to select the best mask per image; the best-performing variant (scorer_v3_4) was chosen by a sweep over the 500-image cohort. The same text recipe was additionally applied to a hand-collected carotid TCAR completion-angiogram test set (4 cases / ~9 clips); these clips have no ground-truth traces, so only detection and mask-area were assessed (no Dice).
abstract.Results: Raw zero-shot SAM 3 segmented arteries from text alone but with high variance: text-only mean Dice was 0.629 (median 0.927, std 0.433) across the 500 Dr.SAM images, with bounding-box-only 0.532 and combined text+bbox 0.632. By stratum, raw text-only Dice was 0.527 (stenosis), 0.655 (aneurysm), and 0.646 (normal); failures were predominantly under-segmentation or confident mislocalization rather than over-segmentation. Adding the anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring/post-processing layer raised mean Dice from 0.629 to 0.933 (median 0.927→0.946; std 0.433→0.064), with 437/500 images reaching Tier A (Dice ≥ 0.90; Tier B 61, C 1, D 1). Post-processed Dice by stratum was 0.916 (stenosis), 0.937 (aneurysm), and 0.936 (normal). On the carotid TCAR clips, image-mode text prompting detected arteries in all clips (per-clip text-prompt scores ~0.354–0.903, median ~0.76), while video arms detected fewer (roughly 33–67% of clips); because no ground-truth traces exist yet, these results are detection/area-only and remain qualitative.
abstract.Conclusions: These preliminary, unpublished results suggest that a frozen, text-promptable foundation model can perform zero-shot arterial segmentation on DSA across vascular territories without fine-tuning, and that a lightweight, anatomy-agnostic post-processing layer can close most of the gap between raw zero-shot output and hand-traced ground truth on an external benchmark. Several caveats temper this: the headline 0.933 mean Dice depends on the candidate-scoring layer (raw zero-shot text-only is 0.629), the carotid/TCAR evaluation has no ground-truth traces and is therefore detection-only and ongoing, and the work is limited to a single external benchmark with no confidence intervals or statistical hypothesis tests reported. The findings motivate, but do not yet establish, a generalizable zero-shot segmentation tool for angiography.

takeaways: Raw, frozen SAM 3 can segment opacified arteries on DSA from text prompts alone, but zero-shot accuracy is modest and highly variable: text-only mean Dice 0.629 (median 0.927, std 0.433) on the 500-image Dr.SAM benchmark — the median is good but a large tail of failures pulls the mean down.

A lightweight, anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring/post-processing layer (no fine-tuning, no extra inference) raises mean Dice from 0.629 to 0.933 and collapses the variance (std 0.433→0.064) — 437/500 images reach Tier A (Dice ≥ 0.90). The 0.933 is a post-processed number, not raw zero-shot.

Across prompt modalities (raw zero-shot), text-only (0.629) and text+bbox (0.632) are comparable and both beat bbox-only (0.532); geometric box prompts alone underperform the model's own text grounding.

Post-processed accuracy is consistent across anomaly strata — stenosis 0.916, aneurysm 0.937, normal 0.936 — with stenosis the hardest (narrower, less-opacified vessels), mirroring the raw zero-shot ordering.

Carotid TCAR completion angiograms are an ongoing stress test only: image-mode text prompting detects arteries in 100% of clips (per-clip scores ~0.354–0.903), but with no ground-truth traces there is no Dice yet — these results are detection/area-only and preliminary.

figures.title: Prompt modality (raw zero-shot, Dr.SAM n=500)
figures.caption: Raw zero-shot SAM 3 mean Dice by prompt arm on the 500-image Dr.SAM benchmark (hand-traced ground truth). Text-only (0.629) and combined text+bbox (0.632) are comparable; bounding-box-only (0.532) trails. No post-processing applied.

figures.title: Post-processing closes the gap
figures.caption: Raw zero-shot text-only vs candidate-scoring/post-processed SAM 3 on the same 500 Dr.SAM images. Mean Dice rises 0.629→0.933 and median 0.927→0.946. The 0.933 includes the anatomy-agnostic post-processing layer (scorer_v3_4); the 0.629 is raw zero-shot text-only.

figures.title: Dice by anomaly stratum (post-processed)
figures.caption: Post-processed (scorer_v3_4) mean Dice by Dr.SAM anomaly stratum (n=500 total: stenosis n=77, aneurysm n=61, normal n=382). Stenosis is hardest; aneurysm and normal are comparable. All values include the post-processing layer.

figures.title: Mask-quality tier distribution (post-processed)
figures.caption: Per-image Dice tier under the post-processed (scorer_v3_4) pipeline on Dr.SAM (n=500). Tier A = Dice ≥ 0.90; B = 0.50–0.90; C = 0–0.50; D = 0.00. 437/500 images reach Tier A; only 2 catastrophic failures remain (C+D). Includes post-processing.

tables.label: Table 1
tables.caption: Raw zero-shot SAM 3 Dice by prompt arm on the Dr.SAM benchmark (n=500; hand-traced ground truth). No post-processing.
tables.note: Dr.SAM (Zohranyan et al., CVPR-W 2024): 500 pelvic-iliac DSA images, hand-traced GT, 1,500 vessel-segment bboxes. All three arms use the same frozen facebook/sam3 checkpoint; only the prompt mode differs. Numbers are raw zero-shot — no candidate-scoring/post-processing.

tables.caption: Raw zero-shot text-only vs post-processed SAM 3 on the same 500 Dr.SAM images, overall and by anomaly stratum.
tables.note: n=500 (stenosis 77, aneurysm 61, normal 382). Tier A = Dice ≥ 0.90. The post-processing layer is an anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring step applied to cached SAM 3 candidate masks (no additional model inference, no fine-tuning). Raw Tier-A count (303) from the Cond 1 failure analysis; post-processed counts from the scorer_v3_4 cohort summary. The 0.933 is NOT a raw zero-shot number.

sections.title: Introduction
paras: Vessel segmentation on digital subtraction angiography (DSA) underpins quantitative angiographic analysis — from completion-angiogram adequacy to stenosis and aneurysm characterization — yet automated approaches have historically depended on anatomy-specific, supervised models trained on institution-specific hand-labeled data, which limits generalization across vascular territories and necessitates retraining when anatomy or acquisition style changes.

Recent promptable segmentation foundation models change this paradigm: SAM 3 (Meta, 2025) accepts natural-language concept prompts and exposes a detection-and-tracking head, enabling segmentation of a named concept without task-specific training. This work tests a narrow, falsifiable claim — that a frozen, text-promptable foundation model can segment opacified arteries on DSA zero-shot, across vascular territories, with no fine-tuning and no per-anatomy prompt retuning — and asks whether a lightweight, anatomy-agnostic post-processing layer can convert that raw capability into accurate masks.

The clinical motivation arose from carotid TCAR completion angiograms, where vessel masks could support automated assessment of completion-angiogram adequacy. Because the hand-collected TCAR clips lack ground-truth traces, the project's quantitative validation centers on the Dr.SAM benchmark (500 pelvic-iliac DSA images with hand-traced ground truth)[1], with TCAR retained as an ongoing, qualitative stress test on messy surgical-field angiography.

We evaluated SAM 3 (facebook/sam3)[2] with weights frozen throughout — no fine-tuning or weight modification was performed at any stage. Three prompt arms were defined: (1) text-only, using a fixed three-prompt "smart-fallback" ladder ("arteries on angiogram" at score threshold 0.3; "opacified arteries on x-ray" at 0.5; "arteries on x-ray" at 0.5; max-score-wins fusion); (2) bounding-box-only, using benchmark-provided vessel bboxes; and (3) combined text+bbox. The text ladder was deliberately modality-level ("arteries on x-ray") rather than anatomy-level — anatomy-specific terms such as "carotid artery" returned no detections in early testing — and was held fixed across territories to preserve the zero-shot, no-retuning framing.

An exploratory video track (SAM 3's video model with single- or peak-frame text prompting and bidirectional propagation through the clip) was run on the TCAR clips to test temporal integration, but the image-mode text arm is the primary recipe carried to external validation.

External validation used the Dr.SAM benchmark[1]: 500 pelvic-iliac DSA images with hand-traced ground-truth masks and bounding-box metadata for 1,500 vessel segments, stratified into stenosis (n=77), aneurysm (n=61), and normal (n=382). For each image, Dice was computed between the predicted and ground-truth masks. The same text recipe used on carotid TCAR was applied unchanged to Dr.SAM — a pure cross-territory transfer test with no pelvic-specific prompt tuning. Dr.SAM (a SAM ViT-H pipeline with bounding-box-prompted iterative refinement and auto-found seed points) provides the model-versus-model reference for the bbox arm.

Failure-mode analysis of the raw text-only run showed that errors were overwhelmingly under-segmentation or confident mislocalization rather than over-segmentation, and that a clean high-quality candidate mask was frequently already present in the model's candidate pool but not selected. We therefore built an anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring layer: a universal geometric/intensity feature module followed by hard gates (e.g. area fraction and solidity bounds) and a soft quality score — a weighted sum of working features, each z-normalized within each image's own candidate pool so that no dataset-global constants are baked in (carrying the scorer to any anatomy). This layer operates post-hoc on cached candidate masks with no additional model inference and no fine-tuning. The best-performing variant on the 500-image cohort, scorer_v3_4 (an area-ramp family scorer chosen by a 72-combination sweep), is reported as the post-processed result.

Limitations of this layer are reported transparently: it is the source of the headline accuracy gain over raw zero-shot output, and its variant selection was tuned on the same external benchmark used for evaluation.

A hand-collected carotid TCAR completion-angiogram test set (4 cases, ~9 clips) was preprocessed from screen-captured MP4 recordings into frame sequences. These clips have no ground-truth vessel traces; consequently, only detection (did a text prompt fire above threshold and produce a non-empty mask) and mask-area were assessed — no Dice. This evaluation is qualitative and ongoing.

Raw zero-shot SAM 3 segmented arteries from text alone but with high variance. Text-only mean Dice was 0.629 (median 0.927, std 0.433); bounding-box-only was 0.532; and combined text+bbox was 0.632 (Table 1, Figure 1). The strong median against the modest mean reflects a bimodal distribution — many near-perfect masks alongside a tail of complete failures. By stratum, raw text-only Dice was 0.527 (stenosis), 0.655 (aneurysm), and 0.646 (normal). Failures were predominantly under-segmentation or confident mislocalization, not over-segmentation.

Applying the anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring/post-processing layer (scorer_v3_4) to the same 500 images raised mean Dice from 0.629 to 0.933 (median 0.927→0.946) and collapsed the variance (std 0.433→0.064) (Table 2, Figure 2). Under the post-processed pipeline, 437/500 images reached Tier A (Dice ≥ 0.90), with 61 in Tier B, 1 in Tier C, and 1 in Tier D (Figure 4) — versus 303 Tier-A images for the raw run. Post-processed Dice was consistent across strata: 0.916 (stenosis), 0.937 (aneurysm), and 0.936 (normal) (Figure 3), with stenosis remaining the hardest stratum.

On the carotid TCAR completion-angiogram clips, image-mode text prompting detected arteries in all clips, with per-clip text-prompt scores ranging from approximately 0.354 to 0.903 (median ~0.76). Video arms detected arteries in fewer clips (roughly one-third to two-thirds). Because these clips have no ground-truth traces, no Dice could be computed; these results are detection/area-only and remain qualitative and ongoing.

sections.title: Discussion
paras: These preliminary results suggest that a frozen, text-promptable foundation model can perform zero-shot arterial segmentation on DSA across vascular territories — the carotid-tuned text recipe transferred unchanged to pelvic-iliac angiography — without any fine-tuning. This is consistent with the project's sharpened thesis: highly accurate zero-shot vessel segmentation of DSA using foundation-model text prompts, with no anatomic specificity and no per-anatomy retuning.

The honest progression is central to interpreting these numbers. Raw zero-shot text-only accuracy was modest and bimodal (mean Dice 0.629, median 0.927). The headline mean Dice of 0.933 — with 437/500 images at Tier A and near-eliminated catastrophic failure — is achieved only after an anatomy-agnostic candidate-scoring/post-processing layer, which adds no model inference and no fine-tuning but is nonetheless the source of most of the accuracy gain. The work should be read as: a foundation model that already places a high-quality vessel mask in its candidate pool for most images, plus a lightweight classical selector that reliably picks it.

Several limitations temper any conclusion. First, the 0.933 depends on the post-processing layer, and the scorer variant was selected on the same external benchmark used to report accuracy. Second, the carotid/TCAR evaluation has no ground-truth traces and is therefore detection-only and ongoing — the original clinical motivation has not yet been quantitatively validated. Third, validation rests on a single external benchmark[1], with no confidence intervals and no statistical hypothesis tests reported here; stenosis remained the hardest stratum, consistent with narrower, less-opacified vessels offering less of the high-contrast tubular signal the model keys on.

Taken together, these findings motivate — but do not yet establish — a generalizable, zero-shot, text-promptable segmentation tool for angiography. Confirmatory next steps include ground-truth tracing for the carotid TCAR set, validation on additional benchmarks and territories, and statistical characterization (confidence intervals, held-out scorer tuning) before any deployment claim.

references: Zohranyan E, Navasardyan V, Navasardyan H, Borodin G, Navasardyan S, Shukla A, et al. Dr.SAM: An efficient framework for automated arteries segmentation and diameter calculation in angiographic images. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Workshops; 2024.
Meta AI. SAM 3: Segment Anything with Concepts. Meta AI Research; 2025. Model: facebook/sam3.
